metadata.title: Build a Case Interview Structuring Coach with Claude Code

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The fourth column, the damage mode, is what I want to build, because the contrast between the model picking a structure for you versus showing three alternatives is the whole point. I want a coach that argues against my framing. Help me: (1) write the anthropic.Anthropic messages.create call that returns three structures with distinct organizing logics plus fit scores and two clarifying questions; (2) design the airline-case prompt so the clarifying questions actually change which structure wins; (3) add the damage-mode column that surfaces what the model says when you ask it to just pick one, so I can see the reflexive default.

I want a coach that argues against my framing. props.segment: Build a Case Interview Structuring Coach

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

The model knows every framework. Profitability, market entry, value chain — it can generate all of them faster than you can think. But it cannot tell you whether your structure fits this problem, or whether it is just the most common structure applied reflexively. What if you could make it argue against your framing before you walk into the room?
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- title card: CASE STRUCTURING COACH; the problem: generic vs. problem-specific structure highlighted

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to write a Python CLI that takes a case prompt and outputs three alternative structures with different organizing logics, rated by how well each fits, plus the two clarifying questions whose answers would change the structure.

props.command:
claude "Write a Python CLI script that takes a business
case prompt (e.g., 'a European airline margins have
compressed 4pp over 2 years') and outputs:
(1) three alternative structures with different
organizing logics, rated by how well each fits this
specific prompt vs. a generic case;
(2) the two most important clarifying questions
whose answers would change which structure is correct.
Use the anthropic Python SDK.
Format output as a clean terminal table."

beats.narration_text: Read the code before trusting it. The script calls Claude with the case prompt and asks for three structures plus two clarifying questions. Verify the SDK call: anthropic dot Anthropic, messages dot create. Verify the output table renders cleanly before running.

props.code:
# case_structure_coach.py -- Claude Code output
import anthropic, sys

def analyze(prompt):
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f"Case: {prompt}\n\nOutput:\n"
            "1) Three alternative structures with "
            "different organizing logics, each rated "
            "1-5 for fit vs generic applicability.\n"
            "2) Two clarifying questions that would "
            "change which structure is correct."}]
    )
    return msg.content[0].text

if __name__ == "__main__":
    case = " ".join(sys.argv[1:]) or \
        "European airline margins compressed 4pp"
    print(analyze(case))

beats.narration_text: Run it on the airline case. Three structures appear: revenue versus cost organizing logic, route-network versus yield-management, and competitive-positioning versus operational-efficiency. Specific fit scores in the second column. The clarifying questions follow: is the margin compression concentrated in one route type, or is it systemic? Has the cost base grown or has revenue per seat fallen?
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording of terminal output: three-structure comparison table with fit scores; two clarifying questions below

beats.narration_text: Now add the fourth column — the damage mode. Ask Claude to add a column showing what the model says when you ask it which structure to use. That contrast — model picks for you versus model shows alternatives — is exactly what the chapter warns against.

props.command:
claude "Modify case_structure_coach.py:
add a fourth column showing what the model says
when asked which structure to use.
Display the contrast: model picks for you vs
model shows alternatives for you to choose."

beats.narration_text: The revised table. Four columns now. The fourth shows the model confidently picking the revenue-versus-cost structure. Below it, the alternatives column shows three options that still need a human judgment. The difference is visible: the model that picks for you has already made the choice the chapter says you must make yourself.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording of revised terminal: four-column table showing model recommendation vs alternatives contrast

beats.narration_text: The model can generate frameworks faster than you can think. The discipline — choosing the structure that fits this problem, not the one that sounds impressive — is exactly what the model cannot do for you. The tool is useful when it shows you options. It becomes a crutch when it makes the choice.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- summary card: model as options-generator vs. model as decision-maker; the chapter's discipline stated

beats.narration_text: Run this on five practice cases before your next interview. Each time the model picks a structure, ask it why the other two are wrong. If it cannot give you a case-specific reason, the pick is generic. The clarifying questions are the real output — they tell you what you do not yet know.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps card: run on 5 cases; interrogate the pick; use clarifying questions as the true output

The discipline — choosing the structure that fits this problem, not the one that sounds impressive — is exactly what the model cannot do for you. The tool is useful when it shows you options. It becomes a crutch when it makes the choice. Run this on five practice cases before your next interview. Each time the model picks a structure, ask it why the other two are wrong. If it cannot give you a case-specific reason, the pick is generic. The clarifying questions are the real output — they tell you what you do not yet know.
